name: Serde (Serialization Framework for Rust)

performance.notes: Zero-copy deserialization possible; near zero overhead with monomorphization

agent_readiness.notes: #[derive(Serialize, Deserialize)] on any struct. serde_json::to_string/from_str. Attributes: #[serde(rename)], #[serde(skip)], #[serde(default)], #[serde(flatten)], #[serde(tag='type')] for enum tagging. Works with 30+ formats: JSON (serde_json), YAML (serde_yaml), TOML (toml), MessagePack (rmp-serde), Bincode, CSV, RON, etc. Zero-copy deserialization with lifetimes. The universal serialization layer for Rust — almost every Rust library depends on it.

gotchas: Complex enum serialization requires careful attribute configuration
serde_json for JSON is separate crate
Lifetime annotations needed for zero-copy deserialization
best_when: All Rust projects that touch JSON, YAML, TOML, or any data format
avoid_when: When compile-time overhead is unacceptable (rarely the case)
summary: Serde is the universal serialization framework for Rust. #[derive(Serialize, Deserialize)] gives any struct JSON/YAML/TOML/binary serialization. serde_json, serde_yaml, toml crates plug in. Attributes for rename, skip, default, flatten, enum tagging. Zero-copy deserialization. Nearly every Rust library depends on Serde — it's the foundational data layer of the Rust ecosystem.
